section.title: The Flight to Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The remnant left after Gedaliah's murder asks Jeremiah to seek God's guidance and vows in advance to obey whatever he says, pleasant or unpleasant. When the answer comes, stay in the land, do not fear Babylon, do not go to Egypt, they reject it outright, accuse Jeremiah and Baruch of manipulation, and drag the whole remnant, prophet and scribe included, down into Egypt anyway.
section.thesis: A remnant that swears in advance to obey whatever God says proves, the moment the answer is unwelcome, that its request for guidance was never sincere, and drags God's own prophet into the very disobedience he warned against.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the remnant seeks guidance and vows total obedience; God's answer through Jeremiah, stay in the land and do not fear, do not go to Egypt; the remnant's outright rejection of that word and forced march to Egypt; and a sign at Tahpanhes that Babylon will follow them even there.

units.label: The remnant seeks guidance, vowing obedience
units.note: a request made in advance of the answer

verses.text: Then all the commanders of the forces, along with Johanan son of Kareah, Jezaniah son of Hoshaiah, and all the people from the least to the greatest, approached
Jeremiah the prophet and said, “May our petition come before you; pray to the LORD your God on behalf of this entire remnant. For few of us remain of the many, as you can see with your own eyes.
Pray that the LORD your God will tell us the way we should walk and the thing we should do.”
“I have heard you,” replied Jeremiah the prophet. “I will surely pray to the LORD your God as you request, and I will tell you everything that the LORD answers; I will not withhold a word from you.”
Then they said to Jeremiah, “May the LORD be a true and faithful witness against us if we do not act upon every word that the LORD your God sends you to tell us.
Whether it is pleasant or unpleasant, we will obey the voice of the LORD our God to whom we are sending you, so that it may go well with us, for we will obey the voice of the LORD our God!”

units.label: God's answer: stay, do not fear, do not go to Egypt
units.note: life and blessing offered, if they will only remain

verses.text: After ten days the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah,
and he summoned Johanan son of Kareah, all the commanders of the forces who were with him, and all the people from the least to the greatest.
Jeremiah told them, “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel, to whom you sent me to present your petition:
‘If you will indeed stay in this land, then I will build you up and not tear you down; I will plant you and not uproot you, for I will relent of the disaster I have brought upon you.
Do not be afraid of the king of Babylon, whom you now fear; do not be afraid of him, declares the LORD, for I am with you to save you and deliver you from him.
And I will show you compassion, and he will have compassion on you and restore you to your own land.’
But if you say, ‘We will not stay in this land,’ and you thus disobey the voice of the LORD your God,
and if you say, ‘No, but we will go to the land of Egypt and live there, where we will not see war or hear the sound of the {{shofar|ram's horn}} or hunger for bread,’
then hear the word of the LORD, O remnant of Judah! This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘If you are determined to go to Egypt and reside there,
then the sword you fear will overtake you there, and the famine you dread will follow on your heels into Egypt, and you will die there.
So all who resolve to go to Egypt to reside there will die by sword and famine and plague. Not one of them will survive or escape the disaster I will bring upon them.’

For this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘Just as My anger and wrath were poured out on the residents of Jerusalem, so will My wrath be poured out on you if you go to Egypt. You will become an object of cursing and horror, of vilification and disgrace, and you will never see this place again.’
The LORD has told you, O remnant of Judah, ‘Do not go to Egypt.’ Know for sure that I have warned you today!
For you have deceived yourselves by sending me to the LORD your God, saying, ‘Pray to the LORD our God on our behalf, and as for all that the LORD our God says, tell it to us and we will do it.’
For I have told you today, but you have not obeyed the voice of the LORD your God in all He has sent me to tell you.
Now therefore, know for sure that by sword and famine and plague you will die in the place where you desire to go to reside.”

units.label: The remnant defies the word, drags Jeremiah to Egypt
units.note: rejecting the very word they vowed to obey

verses.text: When Jeremiah had finished telling all the people all the words of the LORD their God, everything that the LORD had sent him to say,

Azariah son of Hoshaiah, Johanan son of Kareah, and all the arrogant men said to Jeremiah, “You are {{kazab|lying}}! The LORD our God has not sent you to say, ‘You must not go to Egypt to reside there.’
Rather, Baruch son of Neriah is inciting you against us to deliver us into the hands of the Chaldeans, so that they may put us to death or exile us to Babylon!”
So Johanan son of Kareah and all the commanders of the forces disobeyed the command of the LORD to stay in the land of Judah.
Instead, Johanan son of Kareah and all the commanders of the forces took the whole remnant of Judah, those who had returned to the land of Judah from all the nations to which they had been scattered,

the men, the women, the children, the king's daughters, and everyone whom Nebuzaradan captain of the guard had allowed to remain with Gedaliah son of Ahikam, the son of Shaphan, as well as Jeremiah the prophet and Baruch son of Neriah.
So they entered the land of Egypt because they did not obey the voice of the LORD, and they went as far as Tahpanhes.

units.label: A sign at Tahpanhes: Nebuchadnezzar will come
units.note: no refuge lies outside God's word

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah at Tahpanhes:
“In the sight of the Jews, pick up some large stones and bury them in the clay of the brick pavement at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace at Tahpanhes.

Then tell them that this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘I will send for My servant Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and I will set his throne over these stones that I have embedded, and he will spread his royal pavilion over them.
He will come and strike down the land of Egypt, bringing death to those destined for death, captivity to those destined for captivity, and the sword to those destined for the sword.

I will kindle a fire in the temples of the gods of Egypt, and Nebuchadnezzar will burn those temples and take their gods as captives. So he will wrap himself with the land of Egypt as a shepherd wraps himself in his garment, and he will depart from there unscathed.
He will demolish the sacred pillars of the temple of the sun in the land of Egypt, and he will burn down the temples of the gods of Egypt.’”

shofar.gloss: A ram's horn trumpet, sounded to warn of approaching war. The remnant's stated hope in fleeing to Egypt is never again hearing this sound, imagining safety outside the land where God had actually promised to protect them.

kazab.gloss: To lie, to speak falsely. The arrogant men accuse Jeremiah of exactly this, lying, immediately after solemnly vowing to obey whatever true word the LORD would give them, the plainest possible proof that the vow was never sincere.

basic.context: After Gedaliah's murder, the frightened remnant asks Jeremiah to seek God's guidance and swears in advance to obey it completely, whether the answer is pleasant or unpleasant. Ten days later Jeremiah delivers God's clear answer: stay in the land, do not fear Babylon, and above all do not go to Egypt, for those who flee there will die by the very sword and famine they are trying to escape. The remnant's leaders immediately accuse Jeremiah of lying and Baruch of manipulating him, then force the entire remnant, including Jeremiah and Baruch themselves, down into Egypt. At Tahpanhes, God gives a sign that Babylon's king will follow them even there.
basic.hermeneutics: The narrative exposes a universal pattern: a request for guidance made while already resolved on a course of action is not really a request at all. Jeremiah names this exactly, you have deceived yourselves, verse 20, since the vow to obey was tested the moment the answer proved unwelcome and failed instantly. The passage does not merely record disobedience; it records self-deceived disobedience dressed up as piety.
basic.originalLanguages: The people hope in Egypt to escape the sound of the shofar, the ram's horn that signals approaching war, imagining safety where God has promised only that the sword and famine they fear will overtake them. When the answer displeases them, they accuse Jeremiah of kazab, lying, the very charge that exposes their own vow as false, since a true vow to obey cannot survive contact with an unwelcome command.
basic.typology: The remnant's flight to the very place God forbade, seeking safety in Egypt rather than trusting His presence in the land, inverts the Exodus pattern, in which God once led His people out of Egypt toward the promised land; here they flee back into Egypt against His word.
basic.christ: Where Israel's history began with God bringing His people out of Egypt, this remnant reverses that pattern entirely, running back into Egypt out of unbelief rather than trusting the God who promised to build and plant them in the land. Christ, the greater and obedient Son, is Himself carried into Egypt as an infant and brought out again, Matthew 2:15 quoting Hosea 11:1, fulfilling in perfect obedience the pattern Israel and this remnant kept failing to walk in faith.
basic.god: God offers real mercy and even relents of disaster if only the remnant will trust Him and remain, verses 10 to 12, showing His discipline is never merely punitive. Yet He is also entirely truthful in warning what unbelief will cost, and He does not remove the consequences of a choice made against clear revealed guidance, even though He continues to speak to and provide for His people, Jeremiah included, even in their disobedience.
basic.crossReferences: Hosea 11:1 and Matthew 2:15 tie Egypt to both Israel's history and Christ's own infancy. Deuteronomy 17:16 warns the king himself against returning the people to Egypt. James 1:22 calls hearers to be doers of the word, not merely hearers who deceive themselves. Isaiah 30:1-3 similarly condemns seeking refuge in Egypt against God's counsel.
basic.oneStory: A remnant vows to obey God completely, then breaks that vow the instant His answer proves unwelcome, dragging His own prophet down into the very disobedience he warned against. This is the persistent shape of the human heart across the whole Bible, professing obedience while resolved on its own way, a pattern only undone when Christ, the truly obedient Son, walks the path of perfect trust where Israel and this remnant could not.

context.literary: A carefully staged narrative of vow, delay, answer, and betrayal, moving from solemn oath at Jeremiah's tent to the ten day wait for God's word, to outright rejection, to the forced march into Egypt, and finally a prophetic sign delivered in the very land the remnant chose against God's counsel.
context.historical: Set immediately after Gedaliah's assassination, likely late 586 or 585 BC, when the surviving remnant of Judah, fearing Babylonian reprisal for the governor's murder, sought asylum in Egypt, historically a common refuge for fleeing Levantine populations.
context.canonical: A pointed narrative fulfillment of Deuteronomy 17:16's warning against Israel's kings returning the people to Egypt, and a striking inversion of the Exodus itself, God's people fleeing back into the land He once delivered them from.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose that the remnant's professed desire for guidance was insincere from the start, since their vow of total obedience collapsed the moment the answer displeased them, and to record God's clear warning against seeking safety in Egypt against His revealed word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is historical narrative describing one specific act of self-deceived disobedience; it is not a blanket command never to travel to any particular country. Its enduring, transferable principle is that seeking God's guidance while already resolved on a course of action is self-deception, not genuine faith, whatever words are used to ask.

debates.question: Why does God still speak to and care for Jeremiah and Baruch even as they are forced into the very disobedience God warned against?

views.label: Grace toward the innocent caught in others' sin
views.case: Jeremiah and Baruch never chose to go to Egypt; they are physically compelled by armed commanders, and God continues to speak through Jeremiah at Tahpanhes, showing His care follows His faithful servants even into circumstances forced upon them by others' disobedience.

views.label: God's ongoing mercy even toward corporate disobedience
views.case: God's continued speech in Egypt, including a specific sign about Babylon's coming judgment there, shows His willingness to keep warning and instructing His people even in the very place they wrongly fled to, refusing to abandon them entirely to the consequences of their choice.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that God's faithfulness to His word and to His true servants does not depend on human circumstances being ideal, even disobedience forced upon or chosen by others does not silence Him.

study.originalLanguages: The people hope to escape the sound of the shofar, the ram's horn warning of war, by fleeing to Egypt, yet God promises the very sword and famine they dread will follow them there. Their accusation of kazab, lying, against Jeremiah the moment his answer displeases them, is the clearest possible evidence that their earlier oath, whether pleasant or unpleasant we will obey, was never a genuine commitment.

typology.type: The remnant fleeing back into Egypt against God's word, Jeremiah 42:19, 43:7
typology.fulfillment: Inverts the Exodus pattern of deliverance out of Egypt, and stands in contrast to Christ, who is carried into Egypt as an infant in perfect submission to God's protective purpose and brought out again in fulfillment of Hosea 11:1, Matthew 2:15.

study.christ: Israel's story began with God bringing His people out of Egypt in the Exodus, yet here the remnant runs back into Egypt precisely where God forbade it, the pattern of unbelief reversing the pattern of redemption. Matthew 2:15 shows Christ, the true and greater Israel, carried into Egypt in perfect submission to God's protective will and brought out again exactly as Hosea 11:1 had foretold, walking in obedient trust through the very geography where this remnant walked in unbelief.
study.god: God's mercy is remarkably patient, offering to relent of disaster and to build and plant the remnant if they will simply trust Him and remain in the land, verses 10 to 12, showing discipline was never His last word for them. Yet His truthfulness does not bend to comfort self-deceived hearers: He states plainly that the very dangers they flee will overtake them exactly where they run, and He does not spare them the consequences of choosing their own way after being clearly warned, even while He continues mercifully to speak through Jeremiah in the land they wrongly chose.

crossReferences.note: Warns Israel's future king never to make the people return to Egypt, a command this remnant's leaders directly violate by leading the people back there.
Out of Egypt I called My son, applied by Matthew 2:15 to Christ, the perfectly obedient Son who walks the same geography this unbelieving remnant walked in disobedience.
Condemns seeking refuge in Egypt's shadow against the LORD's counsel, calling it a stronghold that will instead bring shame, the same warning realized here.
Be doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves, directly describing the remnant's error of vowing obedience while never intending to keep it.

study.oneStory: A frightened remnant vows total obedience to whatever God says, then breaks that vow the moment His answer proves unwelcome, dragging His own prophet down into the very disobedience he had warned them against. This is the Bible's recurring diagnosis of the human heart, quick to promise obedience and quick to abandon it, a pattern finally broken only by Christ, the obedient Son who walked in perfect trust through the same Egypt this remnant fled to in unbelief.

quiz.q: What do the remnant's leaders promise Jeremiah before he seeks God's guidance for them?

options.text: That they will obey whatever the LORD says through him, whether the word is pleasant or unpleasant.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 record this explicit, solemn vow of total obedience in advance of the answer.

options.text: That they will follow his advice only if it matches their own plans.
options.note: Their stated vow is unconditional obedience, though their actions later prove otherwise.

options.text: That they will consult Egyptian officials as well before deciding.
options.note: No mention is made of consulting Egyptian officials; the vow concerns obedience to the LORD's word alone.

options.text: That they will wait one year before acting on his answer.
options.note: They actually wait only ten days for the answer, and act on their rejection of it almost immediately.

quiz.q: What is God's actual answer to the remnant's request for guidance?

options.text: Stay in the land and do not fear Babylon; if they go to Egypt instead, the very sword and famine they fear will overtake them there.
options.note: Verses 10 to 17 give this exact answer, blessing for staying and disaster for fleeing to Egypt.

options.text: Go immediately to Egypt for safety from Babylon.
options.note: God's answer is the opposite, do not go to Egypt.

options.text: Rebuild Jerusalem's walls before making any decision.
options.note: No instruction about rebuilding walls appears in this passage; the choice is simply stay or flee to Egypt.

options.text: Send messengers to negotiate directly with Nebuchadnezzar.
options.note: God's instruction is not to negotiate but simply to remain in the land and trust Him.

quiz.q: How do the remnant's leaders respond to Jeremiah's answer?

options.text: They accuse him of lying and claim Baruch is manipulating him against them, then force the whole remnant to Egypt.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 and 5 to 7 record this accusation and the forced march to Egypt, including Jeremiah and Baruch themselves.

options.text: They thank him and immediately settle permanently in the land.
options.note: They do the opposite, rejecting his answer and fleeing to Egypt.

options.text: They ask him to seek a second opinion from another prophet.
options.note: No second prophet is consulted; they simply reject Jeremiah's word outright.

options.text: They agree to stay but banish Jeremiah and Baruch from the remnant.
options.note: They do not banish Jeremiah and Baruch; instead they force both of them along to Egypt.

quiz.q: What sign does God give Jeremiah to perform at Tahpanhes in Egypt?

options.text: To bury large stones at the entrance to Pharaoh's palace, symbolizing where Nebuchadnezzar will set his throne when he conquers Egypt.
options.note: Verses 9 to 10 record this specific sign and its meaning.

options.text: To build an altar and offer sacrifices for the remnant's safety.
options.note: No altar or sacrifice is commanded; the sign is burying stones to symbolize Babylon's coming conquest of Egypt.

options.text: To write a letter to Pharaoh warning him personally.
options.note: No letter to Pharaoh is described; the sign is a symbolic act performed in the sight of the Jewish exiles.

options.text: To fast for forty days in mourning for Jerusalem.
options.note: No fast is commanded here; the specific instruction is the symbolic burial of stones at the palace entrance.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah say about the sincerity of the remnant's original request for guidance?

options.text: That they deceived themselves, since they had already resolved not to obey before they ever asked.
options.note: Verse 20 states plainly, you have deceived yourselves, exposing the vow's insincerity.

options.text: That their request was entirely sincere and they simply changed their minds afterward.
options.note: Jeremiah's own assessment is self-deception from the outset, not a later, honest change of mind.

options.text: That only Johanan, not the rest of the people, was insincere.
options.note: The charge of self-deception is leveled at the remnant as a whole, not singled out to Johanan alone.

options.text: That God had actually changed His answer after the first ten days.
options.note: God's answer does not change; what changes is the people's willingness to accept it once it proves unwelcome.

questions: The remnant vowed obedience before hearing God's answer, then rejected it the moment it was unwelcome. Where might you be asking for God's guidance while already resolved on your own plan?
Jeremiah told the remnant plainly that they had deceived themselves. Where in your own life might comfortable self-deception be standing in for genuine obedience?

The people fled to Egypt to escape war and hunger, only to be told the very things they feared would follow them there. Where are you seeking safety in something other than trust in God's actual word to you?

Jeremiah and Baruch were dragged into Egypt against their will by others' disobedience, yet God still spoke to and used Jeremiah there. How does it encourage you that God remains present and active even in circumstances you did not choose?

God's first answer to the remnant was full of mercy, build you up, plant you, show you compassion, if only they would trust and remain. Where is God currently offering you a hard but hopeful path that unbelief is tempting you to reject?

translationNotes.note: The ten day delay before God's answer came is not explained but may reflect the ordinary pattern of prophetic waiting on the LORD, underscoring that God's guidance was neither instant nor to be presumed upon, yet was still given faithfully.

The accusation that Baruch was inciting Jeremiah reflects an existing suspicion of Baruch recorded earlier in chapter 36 and 45, showing a pattern of blaming the scribe for messages the people simply did not want to hear from the prophet himself.

My servant Nebuchadnezzar echoes the identical title used of him in Jeremiah 27:6, underscoring that Babylon's king remains God's appointed instrument of judgment even against Egypt, the very land the remnant fled to for refuge from him.
